1: veath sa qinah ('raise up a lamentation/dirge'): qinah (H7015, formal dirge/elegy) — the distinctive Hebrew meter of 3+2 stresses associated with mourning (first systematically identified in Lamentations). Ezekiel's use of qinah-form for living kings is a prophetic death-announcement in literary form: to be the subject of a qinah is to be declared already dead in the prophetic vision. The form creates the reality it describes.
2: labiya (H3833, 'lioness'): the feminine form of lion, used for the mother who is also a figure of royal power. ben-ariot rabisat ('she lay down among lions'): ari (H738, lion) is the standard word for the mature male lion; labia is the mother. The royal house of Israel as a pride of lions draws on the Judah-lion blessing (Gen 49:9). gur (H1482, 'cub/whelp'): the young lion whose potential for royalty has not yet been tested.
3: vayyilmad litrop terep ('he learned to catch prey/to tear prey'): the education of the young king in the practice of power. tarap (H2963, to tear/rend) — predatory, violent acquisition of power. adam achal ('he devoured men'): the lioness/king who devours his own subjects — the parable indicts the predatory nature of Judah's late monarchy, contrasting with the Good Shepherd of ch 34.
4: vayishmehu goyim bashuachot nilkad ('the nations heard about him; he was caught in their pit'): shuachat (H7825, pit/trap/snare) — the pit that catches the lion is the Babylonian military trap. This refers to Jehoahaz (609 BCE), the first lion-cub, who was taken in chains to Egypt (2 Kgs 23:33-34). The nations that set the trap are Egypt in this first case, contrasting with Babylon in the second.
5: vatere ki nivchelah ('when she saw that her hope was deferred'): yachal (H3176, wait/hope) — the mother-lioness waiting for the return of the first cub, finally abandoning hope. tiqvatah aveda ('her hope was gone/lost'): tiqvah (H8615, hope/cord) — the same word as Rahab's scarlet cord (Josh 2:18): a thread of hope that can be cut. The mother's hope is extinguished by Jehoahaz's fate.
6: The second lion-cub (Jehoiachin or Zedekiah) learns the same predatory pattern. The repetition of the learning-sequence emphasizes the structural failure: not a one-off bad king but an institutional pattern of royal predation. vayyilmad litrop terep — 'he learned to catch prey': the same phrase as v.3, the educational cycle of violence repeating.
7: vayeda almanotav ('he ravaged their strongholds/widow-cities'): almanot can mean 'widows' or 'palaces/citadels' — the text may be deliberately ambiguous. The second interpretation (palaces/strongholds) fits the military context; the first (widows) accuses the king of preying on the most vulnerable. The ambiguity may be intentional: the king who destroys cities leaves behind widows.
8: vayitnu alav goyim miseviv mimdinot ('the nations gathered against him from the surrounding provinces'): the international coalition that closed in on the second cub. The spreading net (resheth, H7568) imagery: the lion is now prey, caught in what had previously been his hunting ground. The reversal of predator-to-prey is the poetic justice of the lamentation.
9: vayyitenuhu basugar bachachim ('they put him in a cage/enclosure in chains'): sugar (H5474, cage/enclosure for dangerous animals) — a royal lion caged is the ultimate humiliation. velo-nishma qolo od al-harei yisrael ('so that his voice would no longer be heard on the mountains of Israel'): the silencing of the royal voice is the defining loss. A king's authority is expressed through his voice commanding the realm; the cage ends the commands. Contrast: the voice of YHWH that speaks through the prophets cannot be caged.
10: imecha kagefen bedamecha ('your mother was like a vine in your vineyard/among the waters'): a second allegory begins: the lioness-mother is now reimagined as a vine. gefen (H1612, vine) — Israel as YHWH's vine is a fundamental OT metaphor (Ps 80:8-16; Isa 5:1-7; Jer 2:21; John 15:1). meme-mayim ('beside/among the waters'): the well-watered vine as image of prosperity — Israel's earlier flourishing under the Davidic monarchy.
11: vayihyu-lo mattot azim ('she had strong/mighty branches/rods'): matteh (H4294, rod/branch/staff/tribe) — the strong branches that serve as rulers' scepters. The word simultaneously means the ruling rod (scepter of authority) and the branch of the vine (natural growth). This double meaning is the allegory's point: the branches that grow naturally also become the instruments of royal rule. shevet moshel ('scepter/rod of a ruler'): shevet (H7626) is both tribal affiliation and governing rod — Gen 49:10's 'the scepter shall not depart from Judah.'
12: vatitash bachema ('she was torn up in anger/fury'): amar (H2534, fury/heat of anger) — divine wrath as the active force behind the Babylonian destruction. veruach haqqadim hobish piryo ('the east wind dried up her fruit'): ruach qadim (east wind) is the consistent metaphor for Babylon in Ezekiel (17:10; 19:12; 27:26) — the wind from the Mesopotamian desert/steppe that scorches and kills vegetation. The Babylonian conquest is the east wind that dries up the Davidic vine.
13: veata shetulatbemdbar bearetz tziyah vetzama ('now she is planted in the wilderness — in a dry and thirsty land'): the transplanted vine in the wilderness is the exile itself. tziyah (H6723, dry ground/drought) and tzama (H6772, thirst) create a merism of waterless desolation, the opposite of the well-watered condition of v.10. The covenant land's fruitfulness is lost; the exile is drought.
14: vatetzeh esh mimmatteh badeha ('fire has spread from one of her branches'): the fire within the vine — internal destruction. This may refer to Zedekiah's rebellion against Babylon, which brought the final conflagration. ein-ba matteh-oz shevet lemoshel ('she has no strong branch left — no scepter/rod to rule with'): the Davidic monarchy's effective end. The lamentation is sealed with hi qinah vatehyi liqinah ('this is a lamentation and has been used as a lamentation'): the meta-statement about the genre — Ezekiel is aware he is composing liturgical mourning-poetry, and declares it will be used as such. The qinah outlives the events it mourns.
